title: Making and applying aerated compost tea
subtitle: Brewing an aerated microbial feed from finished compost and applying it effectively
excerpt: Aerated compost tea uses an aquarium pump to multiply the microbial population in a compost-and-water solution. The result is a liquid feed that delivers microorganisms and dissolved nutrients directly to plant roots and foliage. A 10-litre batch takes 24-36 hours to brew and costs virtually nothing. This explains the brewing method, application timing, and what the evidence says about its effectiveness.
sourceNotes: Soil Biology Primer from NRCS (USDA, public domain) for microbial ecology context. Garden Organic guidance on liquid feeds from compost. Elaine Ingham research on aerated compost tea (referenced for methodology, not verbatim). Jeff Lowenfels 'Teaming with Microbes' (mechanism context).

glossaryTerms.definition: The liquid that drains from the bottom of a compost heap or wormery. Darker than aerated compost tea and should be diluted 10:1 before use.

content.text: Aerated compost tea (ACT) is made by aerating finished compost in water for 24-36 hours. The air feeds the aerobic bacteria and fungi in the compost, causing them to multiply in the liquid. The finished brew has a higher microbial density than a simple compost-water mix. When applied, it delivers those microorganisms to soil and foliage. The dissolved nutrients are a side benefit. The microbial content is the main claimed value.

A note on the evidence: aerated compost tea has a mixed research record. Under controlled conditions, beneficial effects on plant growth and disease suppression have been observed in some studies but not reliably replicated across different compost sources, target plants, and application conditions. The effect, if any, depends heavily on the quality of the compost used. Good compost makes good tea; poor compost makes poor tea. Treat this as a low-cost enhancement to good composting practice, not a replacement for it.
Equipment
10-litre bucket, clean and food-grade or dedicated to the purpose.
Aquarium air pump: a standard small aquarium pump (12-18 litres per minute) is sufficient for a 10-litre batch.
Air diffuser stone: attached to the air pump line and placed at the bucket base to disperse bubbles.
Permeable bag or old tights for holding the compost: allows the brew to flow through while keeping the compost mass contained.
Brewing sequence
Fill the bucket with 9 litres of water. If using tap water, aerate for 30 minutes first to off-gas chlorine, or leave tap water standing overnight.
Add 200-300 g of finished compost in the permeable bag. Submerge in the bucket.
Optional: add 10-15 ml of unsulphured molasses as a microbial food source. This accelerates bacterial multiplication. Do not use sulphured molasses (antifungal properties).
Run the air pump continuously for 24-36 hours at 18-24°C. Below 15°C, the microbial activity is slower; brew for 36-48 hours.
The finished brew should smell sweet or earthy, not sour or sulphurous. A sour smell indicates anaerobic conditions; insufficient aeration or too much molasses.
Apply immediately: ACT begins losing its microbial activity within 4 hours of stopping the pump. Do not store.
Application
Soil drench: apply at the root zone at 1 litre per square metre. Apply in the morning so the soil surface can dry before night.
Foliar spray: dilute 1:1 with water and apply to leaves in early morning. Strain well through a fine mesh before loading a sprayer to avoid blocking the nozzle.
Frequency: once every two to three weeks during the growing season. There is no known harm from more frequent application, but diminishing returns beyond this frequency.
Simple compost leachate vs ACT
Compost
leachate

(the liquid that drains from the bottom of a wormery or compost heap) is not the same as aerated compost tea. It is nutrient-rich but potentially anaerobic and lower in microbial diversity. Dilute 10:1 before applying to plants; do not use undiluted. ACT is more labour-intensive but theoretically higher in beneficial microbial activity.

attrs.symptom: Brew smells sour or of sulphur
attrs.cause: Anaerobic conditions developed during brewing; insufficient aeration relative to the compost load or molasses quantity
attrs.fix: Discard this batch. For the next brew, reduce compost to 150 g, reduce or eliminate molasses, and check the pump airflow is reaching the bucket bottom.

attrs.symptom: Plants show no response after several applications
attrs.cause: The compost used as the source material was poor quality or partially composted rather than finished
attrs.fix: Use only well-finished, earthy-smelling compost as the tea source. The quality of the compost determines the quality of the tea.
